chunk_summary: Histology-based long-term monitoring in EoE involves periodic endoscopy to assess remission and guide therapy adjustments, with individualized intervals and pragmatic strategies for resource-limited settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of histology in long-term monitoring of eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE).
Learn the recommended timelines for endoscopic reassessment during induction and maintenance therapy.
Identify strategies to optimize monitoring in resource-limited settings.
Recognize the importance of individualized treatment plans in EoE management.
key_facts: Histology and symptoms correlate imperfectly in EoE, necessitating periodic objective reassessment.
Endoscopy with biopsies is recommended 8–12 weeks after induction therapy initiation, or at least 4 weeks for elemental diets.
Maintenance therapy is advised after achieving histologic remission to prevent relapse and esophageal remodeling.
Stable remission may warrant endoscopic evaluation every 1–3 years, with earlier reassessment if symptoms like dysphagia or food impaction recur.
In resource-limited settings, step-up elimination strategies (e.g., milk-only or 2–4 food approaches) and validated symptom tools are recommended to reduce endoscopy burden.
reference_quote: After starting induction therapy (diet, swallowed topical steroids, or PPI), repeat endoscopy with biopsies is typically planned after about 8–12 weeks to assess histologic response; for elemental diets, reassessment should not be earlier than about 4 weeks.
